title: Neck Pain
authors: Fan Jiang, Soichiro Takamiya, Michael G. Fehlings

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Neck Pain
items.content: Neck pain is a prevalent issue globally, ranking as the fourth leading cause of disability in the United States. It affects nearly half of the population at some point in their lives, with higher incidence rates among women and older individuals. Various risk factors contribute to neck pain, including sports and work-related injuries, low job satisfaction, poor support at work, genetics, headaches, sleep disorders, smoking, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, secondary gain, history of neck or back pain, and overall physical or mental health.
keyPoints: Fourth leading cause of disability in the U.S.
Lifetime prevalence is nearly 50%
Higher risk for women
Incidence increases with age, peaking in late middle life

items.title: Identifying Neuropathic Components in Neck Pain
items.content: When evaluating neck pain, it is crucial to determine if there is a neuropathic component, which suggests cervical radiculopathy. This type of pain arises from identifiable nerves and can be caused by compression or irritation of cervical spinal nerves due to disk herniation, ligamentous hypertrophy, or facet overgrowth.
keyPoints: Neuropathic neck pain indicates cervical radiculopathy
Causes include compression/irritation of cervical spinal nerves
Common etiologies: disk herniation, ligamentous hypertrophy, facet overgrowth

items.title: Identifying Red Flags in Neck Pain
items.content: A complete clinical history is crucial for identifying red flags that may indicate serious underlying conditions. Key concerns include a history of cancer, fever, and weight loss, which should prompt investigations to rule out malignancy or infectious etiologies.
keyPoints: History of cancer, fever, and weight loss are red flags prompting further investigation.
Red flags necessitate ruling out malignancy and infections.

items.title: Assessing Radiculopathy in Neck Pain
items.content: Radicular pain often follows dermatomal distributions, suggesting neuropathic origins. A complete neurologic examination is required if radiculopathy-like symptoms are present, including assessment of motor strength, sensation, and reflexes.
keyPoints: Radicular pain typically follows dermatomal patterns.
Neurological exam includes cranial nerves, motor strength, sensation, coordination, reflexes, gait.

items.title: Imaging Modalities for Neck Pain
items.content: MRI is the gold standard for assessing soft tissue structures in neck pain and is indicated for radiculopathy or myelopathy. CT scans are useful for evaluating fractures, while x-rays provide initial assessments for spinal pathologies.
keyPoints: MRI is preferred for soft tissue assessment and when suspecting malignancy or infection.
CT scan is used for fracture concerns and hardware complications.
X-rays screen for fractures, instability, and osteoarthritis.

items.title: When to Use EMG/NCS for Neck Pain with Neuropathic Symptoms
items.content: Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies (NCS) are valuable when a patient's symptoms do not align with MRI findings, helping differentiate between radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and brachial plexopathy.
keyPoints: Use EMG/NCS when clinical suspicion is high but electrodiagnostic findings are negative.
Referral to a neurologist recommended in such cases.
Helps distinguish spinal radiculopathy from other neuropathies.

items.title: Understanding Cervical Radiculopathy and Its Common Causes
items.content: Cervical radiculopathy is often caused by herniated disks, osteophytic spurs, or narrowing intervertebral foramen. Ischemia and hypoxia of the nerve root are key mechanisms.
keyPoints: Herniated cervical disks cause ~25% of cases.
~70% due to protruding disks or narrowing from osteophytes/hypertrophic joints.
C7 is most frequently affected, followed by C6.
Symptoms include neck pain, sensory disturbance, and motor weakness.

items.title: Recognizing Early Signs of Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy
items.content: Early signs like paresthesia or minor loss of hand dexterity can be overlooked. Prompt diagnosis and intervention are crucial for better outcomes.
keyPoints: Initial symptoms can be mild, such as paresthesia or clumsiness.
Delay in diagnosis can be up to 2 years.
Prompt surgical intervention leads to better outcomes.
Deterioration is typically slow and progressive.

items.title: Neck Pain in Spinal Metastases: Common Presentation and Pathological Fractures
items.content: Spinal metastases, although involving the cervical spine in less than 20% of cases, are the most common cause of neck pain due to extradural spinal tumors. This pain is typically unrelieved by rest and worsens at night. Additionally, metastatic involvement can lead to pathological fractures contributing to neck pain.
keyPoints: Spinal metastases are the most common extradural spinal tumors causing neck pain.
<20% of patients with spinal metastatic tumors have cervical spine involvement.
Neck pain from metastases is unrelieved by rest and worsens at night.
Pathological fractures due to metastases can cause neck pain.

items.title: Cervical Epidural Abscess: Urgent Diagnosis and Intervention
items.content: A cervical epidural abscess presents with severe neck pain and progressive neurologic deterioration due to cord compression, necessitating urgent surgical intervention. This condition can be mistaken for stroke but is distinguished by neck pain aggravated by palpation of the spinous process.
keyPoints: Cervical epidural abscess causes severe neck pain and neurologic deterioration.
Urgent surgical intervention is required due to cord compression.
Neck pain aggravated by palpation of the spinous process helps distinguish it from stroke.

items.title: Chiari Type 1 Malformations and Neck Pain: Understanding the Pathophysiology
items.content: Chiari type 1 malformations can cause headache and neck pain, which are exacerbated by coughing. The exact pathophysiological mechanism is unclear but may involve a dissociation between intracranial and intraspinal pressures or traction on pain-sensitive nerves of the dura mater.
keyPoints: Chiari type 1 malformations cause headache and neck pain aggravated by cough.
Possible mechanisms include dissociation between intracranial and intraspinal pressures, or traction on pain-sensitive nerves.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis and Cervical Spine Involvement: Recognizing the Risks of Atlantoaxial Subluxation
items.content: Rheumatoid arthritis frequently affects the cervical spine, leading to neck pain primarily due to cervical instability. About 50% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis experience atlantoaxial subluxation, which can result in neurologic deterioration.
keyPoints: Rheumatoid arthritis commonly involves the cervical spine, causing neck pain from cervical instability.
Approximately 50% of rheumatoid arthritis patients have atlantoaxial subluxation, risking neurologic deterioration.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.title: Management of Acute Non-Radiculopathic Neck Pain
items.content: Acute neck pain without radiculopathy or other neurologic findings typically resolves spontaneously. Treatment focuses on promoting rapid return to normal function and providing pain relief while healing. Common treatments include NSAIDs, acetaminophen, cold packs, heat, and cyclobenzaprine for muscle spasm and drowsiness.
keyPoints: Spontaneous improvement is common in acute neck pain without radiculopathy.
NSAIDs, acetaminophen, cold/heat therapy, and cyclobenzaprine (5–10 mg) are typical treatments.
Educate patients on the favorable natural history to avoid unnecessary imaging.
Physiotherapy including exercises like shoulder rolls and neck stretches can be effective.
Soft neck collars and massage may provide temporary relief.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.title: Approach to Chronic Non-Radiculopathic Neck Pain
items.content: Chronic neck pain without radiculopathy or neurologic findings benefits from supervised exercise programs and may respond to acupuncture. Spinal manipulation is not recommended due to lack of efficacy and potential risk. Surgery is rarely indicated unless there are specific conditions like spine instability or fractures.
keyPoints: Supervised exercise programs can improve function and relieve symptoms in chronic neck pain.
Acupuncture may provide short-term benefits compared to sham procedures.
Spinal manipulation alone is not effective and carries injury risks.
Surgery is limited to cases with specific conditions like instability or fractures.
Referral to a specialist for radiofrequency ablation of cervical facet joints can be considered if conservative treatments fail.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.title: Initial Management of Acute Neck Pain with Radiculopathy
items.content: For acute neck pain with radiculopathy due to disk disease, initial management involves NSAIDs, acetaminophen, muscle relaxants, and activity modification. Gentle exercise is recommended while avoiding inactivity.
keyPoints: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or both are reasonable as initial therapy.
Avoidance of activities that trigger symptoms is advised.
Gentle supervised exercise and avoidance of inactivity are beneficial.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.title: Indications for Surgical Intervention in Cervical Radiculopathy
items.content: Surgical decompression is considered for cervical radiculopathy due to bony compression from spondylosis, especially with progressive motor deficit, functionally limiting pain unresponsive to conservative management, or spinal cord compression.
keyPoints: Consider surgical decompression if there's a progressive motor deficit.
Indicated for functionally limiting pain that fails conservative treatment.
Spinal cord compression is an indication for surgery.
Risk of subsequent radiculopathy or myelopathy post-fusion: ~3% per year, 26% per decade.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as a risk factor for neck pain in the passage?
items.explanation: The passage mentions genetics, low job satisfaction, and obesity as risk factors but not high physical activity.

items.question: What is the typical characteristic of neck pain arising from cervical spine diseases?
options: It is accompanied by focal tenderness
items.explanation: Neck pain from cervical spine diseases typically arises with movement and may be accompanied by focal tenderness.

items.question: What should a clinician identify when evaluating neck pain?
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes identifying if the neck pain has a neuropathic component, indicating cervical radiculopathy.

items.question: Which maneuver is performed by passively rotating and flexing a patient's neck laterally and applying axial compression to test for radiculopathy originating from the cervical spine?
items.explanation: Spurling's maneuver is used to test for radiculopathy originating from the cervical spine.

items.question: What imaging modality is considered the gold standard for assessing soft tissue structures in patients with neck pain?
items.explanation: MRI is the gold standard for assessing soft tissue structures in patients with neck pain.

items.question: Which test involves snapping the distal phalanx of the middle finger to check for hyperreflexia?
items.explanation: Hoffmann's test is performed by snapping the distal phalanx of the middle finger to check for hyperreflexia.

items.question: What does a positive Lhermitte's sign indicate when elicited by gently flexing a patient's neck?
items.explanation: A positive Lhermitte's sign indicates underlying cervical spinal pathology.

items.question: Which nerve root is most frequently affected by cervical radiculopathy?
items.explanation: The C7 nerve root is the most frequently affected in cases of cervical radiculopathy.

items.question: What percentage of cervical radiculopathy is caused by herniated cervical disks?
items.explanation: Herniated cervical disks cause about 25% of cervical radiculopathy.

items.question: Which diagnostic studies are often utilized to distinguish between radiculopathy and peripheral neuropathy in patients with neck pain?
items.explanation: Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies (NCS) are used to distinguish radiculopathy from peripheral neuropathy.

items.question: What is a common cause of neck pain in patients with cervical disk herniation?
items.explanation: Neck pain from cervical disk herniation is often due to ischemia of the compressed root and/or spinal cord, and inflammation arising from degenerative disks.

items.question: Which type of spinal tumor is most commonly associated with neck pain due to metastases?
items.explanation: Metastases are the most common extradural spinal tumors causing neck pain.

items.question: What symptom is often absent in cervical vertebral osteomyelitis, potentially leading to a delayed diagnosis?
items.explanation: Fever can be absent in patients with cervical vertebral osteomyelitis, causing diagnostic delays.

items.question: Which vascular pathology is characterized by sudden neck pain that may be aggravated by palpation of the spinous process?
items.explanation: Cervical epidural hematomas present with sudden neck pain that can be aggravated by palpation of the spinous process.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by dissociation between intracranial and intraspinal pressures, potentially causing headache and neck pain aggravated by cough?
items.explanation: Chiari type 1 malformations can cause headache and neck pain due to dissociation between intracranial and intraspinal pressures.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.question: In which condition is cervical instability a common cause of neck pain, potentially leading to neurologic deterioration?
items.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis commonly affects the cervical spine, causing neck pain due to cervical instability.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.question: Which condition is associated with neck pain that tends to be worse at rest and affects the cervical spine less commonly than thoracolumbar regions?
items.explanation: Paget's disease can cause neck pain, which tends to be worse at rest and is less common in the cervical spine.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.question: Which of the following is a hallmark of myofascial pain?
options: Palpable nodules in taut muscle bands
items.explanation: Myofascial trigger points, which are palpable nodules housed inside taut muscle bands, are regarded as a hallmark of myofascial pain.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.question: What is the usual goal of therapy for patients with acute neck pain?
options: To prevent muscle spasms using cyclobenzaprine
To promote a rapid return to normal function and provide pain relief
To immediately refer to a specialist
items.explanation: The usual goals of therapy for acute neck pain are to promote a rapid return to normal function and provide pain relief while healing proceeds.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.question: Which treatment has not been shown to be effective for chronic neck pain?
items.explanation: Spinal manipulation alone has not been shown to be effective for chronic neck pain and carries a risk for injury.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.question: What is a reasonable initial therapy for acute neck pain with radiculopathy due to disk disease?
options: A course of NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or both, with or without muscle relaxants
High-dose oral glucocorticoids as the first line treatment
items.explanation: Initial therapy includes NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or both, with or without muscle relaxants.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.question: Which of the following is a potential risk associated with epidural steroid injections in the neck?
items.explanation: Epidural steroid injections in the neck carry a risk of spinal cord injury.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.question: What is indicated for cervical radiculopathy due to bony compression from cervical spondylosis with foraminal narrowing?
options: Periodic follow-up and consideration of surgical decompression
Immediate use of high-dose oral glucocorticoids
items.explanation: Periodic follow-up to assess for progression and consideration of surgical decompression are indicated.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.statement: Neck pain is more prevalent in men than women.
items.explanation: The passage states that women appear to be at a higher risk for neck pain than men.

items.statement: Neck pain can sometimes be confused with shoulder or peripheral nerve issues.
items.explanation: Pain from the brachial plexus, shoulder, or peripheral nerves can mimic cervical spine disease.

items.statement: Cervical spine x-rays are useful for evaluating the spinal cord and ruling out intervertebral disk pathologies.
items.explanation: Cervical spine x-rays have poor visualization of soft tissues, making them less useful for evaluating the spinal cord or intervertebral disks.

items.statement: MRI is indicated for patients presenting with radiculopathy symptoms or signs of myelopathy.
items.explanation: MRI is the preferred imaging modality for assessing radiculopathy and myelopathy.

items.statement: Routine laboratory tests are essential for assessing most patients with neck pain.
items.explanation: Routine laboratory tests have limited value in the assessment of most patients with neck pain.

items.statement: Degenerative cervical myelopathy can present with severe dysfunction such as gait disturbance and quadriparesis.
items.explanation: Severe dysfunction including gait disturbance, quadriparesis, and bowel or bladder incontinence often presents at a later stage of degenerative cervical myelopathy.

items.statement: Neck pain from cervical epidural abscesses is typically relieved with rest.
items.explanation: Cervical epidural abscess causes severe neck pain requiring urgent intervention, not relieved by rest.

items.statement: Meningitis can cause neck pain accompanied by fever and headache.
items.explanation: Meningitis is an important cause of neck pain, usually with accompanying fever and headache.

items.statement: Approximately 50% of patients with whiplash injury will have persistent neck pain for more than a year.
items.explanation: About 50% of patients with whiplash injury experience persistent neck pain for one year.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.statement: Ankylosing spondylitis causes neck pain that typically worsens with activity.
items.explanation: Neck pain from ankylosing spondylitis usually worsens with rest or inactivity.
items.reference: Neck Pain · CONGENITAL | AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY | ENDOCRINE/METABOLIC | TRAUMATIC

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.statement: Surgery is often the first-line treatment for acute neck pain without neurologic findings.
items.explanation: Surgery plays a very limited role in patients with acute and chronic neck pain without neurologic findings.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.statement: Massage can provide temporary pain relief for neck pain.
items.explanation: Massage is mentioned as a treatment that can produce temporary pain relief.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.statement: Opioid analgesics can be used in the emergency department and for short courses as an outpatient.
items.explanation: Opioids are appropriate for short-term use in managing acute pain.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.statement: Hard cervical collars are generally well-tolerated and recommended for long-term use.
items.explanation: Hard collars are poorly tolerated and not typically recommended.
items.reference: Neck Pain · NECK PAIN WITH RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS

items.assertion: Neck pain is often associated with nonspecific and vague symptoms.
items.reason: Patients frequently present with occipital headache as a common complaint.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why neck pain has nonspecific and vague symptoms.

items.assertion: Spurling's maneuver is used to diagnose cervical spinal cord pathology.
items.reason: It tests for radiculopathy originating from the cervical spine by triggering or worsening symptoms of upper extremity radiculopathy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as Spurling's maneuver specifically tests for radiculopathy, not spinal cord pathology.

items.assertion: CT myelography can be used when MRI is contraindicated.
items.reason: It involves injecting contrast into the spinal canal prior to a CT scan to assess the spinal cord and surrounding structures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CT myelography is used when MRI is contraindicated.

items.assertion: Early signs of cervical myelopathy can be mild and easily overlooked.
items.reason: The early signs include paresthesia, minor loss of hand dexterity, or feelings of 'clumsiness'.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early signs can be easily overlooked.

items.assertion: Degenerative cervical myelopathy typically progresses rapidly.
items.reason: The condition often leads to severe dysfunction such as gait disturbance and quadriparesis.
items.explanation: While the assertion is false, the reason is true; degenerative cervical myelopathy typically progresses slowly.

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
items.assertion: Acupuncture provides long-term benefits for chronic neck pain.
items.reason: Acupuncture provided short-term benefit for some patients when compared to a sham procedure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because acupuncture only provides short-term benefits, not long-term.
items.reference: Neck Pain · ☐ OTHER CAUSES | Neck Pain | NECK PAIN WITHOUT RADICULOPATHY OR OTHER NEUROLOGIC FINDINGS
